content.text: Language models do not read text the way humans do — they decompose strings into discrete units called tokens before any learning or inference can happen. The choice of tokenization algorithm determines how efficiently vocabulary space is used, how well rare and morphologically complex words are represented, and even whether a model can handle code, math, or multilingual text gracefully. Subword tokenization, with Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) as its most prominent representative, is the family of algorithms that struck the right balance for modern large language models.

Subword tokenization is a class of text-segmentation strategies that split words into smaller units — neither whole words nor individual characters — in a data-driven way. The resulting vocabulary contains a mixture of common whole words, common morphemes (un-, -ing, -ness), and character-level fallbacks for truly rare strings. Every sequence of Unicode text can be encoded without an out-of-vocabulary (OOV) token because the character or byte level provides an unconditional fallback.

The three dominant subword algorithms are Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), WordPiece (used by BERT and its derivatives), and Unigram Language Model / SentencePiece (used by T5, LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, and others). All three share the same top-level goal — a compact vocabulary that generalises to unseen words — but differ in how they learn and apply the segmentation. BPE is the most widely explained entry point because its algorithm is the simplest to trace step by step.

Byte-Pair Encoding as applied to NLP was introduced by Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch in their 2016 paper on neural machine translation. It adapted a 1994 data-compression algorithm: scan a symbol sequence for the most frequent adjacent pair, replace every occurrence of that pair with a new single symbol, and record the substitution rule. Applied iteratively to a text corpus, BPE surfaces the sub-word units that occur most productively in the language, starting from individual characters and ending at a specified vocabulary size.

The tokenizer is the first and last transformation in every LLM pipeline. At training time it maps raw text to integer IDs fed to the embedding layer. At inference time the model's output logits are argmaxed over the vocabulary, and the resulting IDs are decoded back to text. Any mistake or inefficiency in the tokenizer propagates to every downstream component.

The naive alternatives fail at opposite extremes. Word-level vocabularies require listing every word form in the training corpus, producing vocabularies of millions of entries with enormous OOV rates for morphologically rich languages like Finnish, Turkish, or German. Character-level vocabularies produce extremely long sequences — a 1,000-character document becomes a 1,000-token sequence — which is computationally expensive because attention is quadratic in sequence length. Subword tokenization is the practical middle ground: typical English text achieves roughly 3–5 characters per token, giving a 3–5× sequence-length reduction over character-level at the cost of a vocabulary of 32 000–128 000 entries.

The choice of vocabulary size and tokenization algorithm also directly affects how many tokens a prompt consumes, which has cost implications for API users and memory implications during training. A poorly chosen tokenizer that fragments a programming language's keywords into many small pieces degrades code-generation benchmarks measurably.
Open vocabulary: every byte sequence — including emoji, code, and new coinages — is representable because the byte or character level serves as an unconditional fallback.
emerge as tokens, allowing the model to share parameters across morphologically related words.
Sequence efficiency: common words become single tokens, keeping sequence lengths manageable for self-attention.
Multilingual capability: a single byte-level BPE vocabulary can cover dozens of languages simultaneously with a modest increase in vocabulary size.
Compact vocabulary: vocabularies of 32k–128k entries are small enough for embedding tables and output projection layers to be tractable in memory.
Deterministic encoding: given the same merge rule list, the same input always produces the same token sequence, which makes training and evaluation reproducible.

Context-sensitivity: the same surface string can tokenize differently depending on surrounding context (e.g., capitalisation or preceding whitespace affects GPT-4 tokenization), making it hard to reason about token boundaries when debugging prompts.
may be split at arbitrary digit boundaries, causing the model to have no explicit positional information about digit significance.

Language imbalance: vocabularies trained predominantly on English will fragment non-Latin scripts into many small pieces, inflating token counts and effectively reducing context windows for those languages.

Training corpus dependency: the tokenizer vocabulary is fixed at training time and cannot be extended without retraining the full model, which creates a hard dependency between tokenizer and model weights.

Greedy decoding artefacts: BPE applies merges greedily; a different permutation of the same characters might tokenize to fewer tokens, so the encoding is not globally optimal. SentencePiece's Unigram model addresses this but adds complexity.
Opaque boundaries: unlike word-level tokenization, subword boundaries are not linguistically meaningful, which complicates tasks like named-entity recognition that need character-offset alignment.
The BPE training procedure begins with a corpus-level word-frequency dictionary. Each word is split into its constituent characters, and a special end-of-word marker (commonly

Formally, let the initial symbol vocabulary be the set of distinct characters in the corpus plus the end-of-word marker. The algorithm maintains a representation of the corpus as a sequence of symbol sequences. At each step:
Count the frequency of every adjacent symbol pair across the entire corpus, weighted by word frequency.
to the vocabulary. Repeat until the vocabulary reaches the target size.
The number of merge operations performed equals
is the target vocabulary size and
is the size of the initial character alphabet. A typical English BPE tokenizer might start with ~256 bytes as base symbols and perform ~49 744 merges to reach a vocabulary of 50 000 tokens.

Encoding an unseen string at inference time applies the learned merge rules greedily in the exact order they were learned. The input word is split into characters, then each merge rule is applied whenever the corresponding pair appears, in order from rule 1 to rule N. This deterministic greedy replay is very fast — O(n·m) where n is the string length and m is the number of merge rules, but in practice the inner loop terminates early because only a small fraction of rules fire for any given word.

WordPiece, used by BERT, differs from BPE in its merge criterion. Instead of selecting the pair with the highest raw frequency, WordPiece selects the pair that maximises the likelihood of the training data under a language model:

attrs.latex: ext{score}(a, b) =  rac{	ext{freq}(ab)}{	ext{freq}(a) \cdot 	ext{freq}(b)}

content.text: This normalisation by individual token frequency makes WordPiece prefer merges that are informative — pairs whose joint frequency exceeds what would be expected from their marginal frequencies. WordPiece also uses a distinct continuation prefix (

The Unigram approach starts from a large initial vocabulary and iteratively removes tokens whose removal causes the smallest drop in corpus log-likelihood under a unigram language model. Encoding uses the Viterbi algorithm to find the segmentation that maximises the probability of the input under the learned unigram distribution:
is the set of all valid segmentations of input
is the unigram probability of subword
. Unlike BPE, the Unigram model can produce multiple valid segmentations with associated probabilities, enabling regularisation through subword sampling during training.
The diagram below illustrates the complete subword tokenization pipeline, from raw text corpus through the training loop to the final inference-time encoding step.

attrs.alt: Subword tokenization pipeline architecture — BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece

content.text: The pipeline has two distinct phases. During training, a corpus is pre-tokenised by whitespace and punctuation into word-level strings, each of which is split into its base symbols. The BPE merge loop then runs repeatedly: count all adjacent symbol-pair frequencies across the corpus, select the most frequent pair, merge it everywhere, record the rule, and extend the vocabulary. After the target vocabulary size is reached, the ordered merge rule list and the final vocabulary are serialised to disk (the tokenizer files).

During inference, the same pre-tokenisation step splits input text into candidate words, each is split into base symbols, and the recorded merge rules are replayed in order. The resulting symbol sequence is mapped through the vocabulary to integer IDs. The decoder performs the inverse: IDs are looked up in the vocabulary, continuation markers are stripped, and whitespace is restored.
Subword tokenization is the default choice for any large language model or sequence-to-sequence model trained on natural language or mixed natural-language-and-code text. It is appropriate whenever:
The model must handle open-domain text including proper nouns, neologisms, and technical jargon that cannot be exhaustively listed in a word-level vocabulary.
The training corpus spans multiple languages or scripts, making a shared subword vocabulary more efficient than separate vocabularies per language.
The model is trained from scratch, so the tokenizer can be fit to the specific corpus distribution.
You are fine-tuning or prompting a pre-existing model — in which case you must use the tokenizer that was paired with that model's weights, since they are jointly optimised.

Character-level tokenization is preferred for very small models trained on specific character-sensitive tasks (e.g., spelling correction, character-level generation of musical notation). Pure word-level tokenization is largely obsolete for LLMs but still appears in older classification models where a fixed vocabulary is acceptable and rare words are simply mapped to a single

A rule of thumb: if the expected vocabulary of your domain can be enumerated and is stable (e.g., a 50-token chess notation alphabet), use that fixed vocabulary directly. If the domain is open-ended or multilingual, use subword tokenization with a vocabulary size between
, depending on language diversity and compute budget.
The simplest path to a working BPE tokenizer is the Hugging Face
library, which implements BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram in Rust with Python bindings. Training a new tokenizer from scratch on a custom corpus takes only a few lines:

from tokenizers import Tokenizer
from tokenizers.models import BPE
from tokenizers.trainers import BpeTrainer
from tokenizers.pre_tokenizers import ByteLevel
from tokenizers.decoders import ByteLevel as ByteLevelDecoder

# 1. Initialise a BPE model
tokenizer = Tokenizer(BPE(unk_token="[UNK]"))

# 2. Byte-level pre-tokeniser: handles any Unicode without OOV
tokenizer.pre_tokenizer = ByteLevel(add_prefix_space=False)
tokenizer.decoder = ByteLevelDecoder()

# 3. Configure the trainer
trainer = BpeTrainer(
    vocab_size=32_000,
    min_frequency=2,          # ignore pairs seen fewer than 2 times
    special_tokens=["[UNK]", "[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[PAD]", "[MASK]"],
    show_progress=True,
)

# 4. Train on one or more text files
files = ["train.txt"]          # line-per-document plain text
tokenizer.train(files, trainer)

# 5. Save vocabulary and merges
tokenizer.save("my_bpe_tokenizer.json")

# 6. Round-trip test
tokenizer2 = Tokenizer.from_file("my_bpe_tokenizer.json")
encoding = tokenizer2.encode("unhappiness")
print(encoding.tokens)         # e.g. ['un', 'happi', 'ness']
print(encoding.ids)            # e.g. [1204, 37452, 897]
print(tokenizer2.decode(encoding.ids))  # 'unhappiness'
For integration with the Transformers library, wrap the trained tokenizer in a
and add any model-specific special token handling:

from transformers import PreTrainedTokenizerFast

tokenizer = PreTrainedTokenizerFast(
    tokenizer_file="my_bpe_tokenizer.json",
    bos_token="<s>",
    eos_token="</s>",
    unk_token="[UNK]",
    pad_token="[PAD]",
    model_max_length=2048,
)

# Now usable with any Transformers model/trainer
inputs = tokenizer(
    "Hello, world!",
    return_tensors="pt",
    truncation=True,
    padding="max_length",
)
print(inputs["input_ids"].shape)   # torch.Size([1, 2048])
Minimal BPE from scratch in pure Python
The following is a self-contained, runnable implementation of the BPE training loop. It omits performance optimisations intentionally, so each step of the algorithm is visible:

from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple

def get_vocab(corpus: str) -> Dict[str, int]:
    """Build word-frequency dict; each word is space-separated chars + </w>."""
    vocab: Dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)
    for word in corpus.split():
        vocab[" ".join(list(word)) + " </w>"] += 1
    return dict(vocab)


def get_stats(vocab: Dict[str, int]) -> Dict[Tuple[str, str], int]:
    """Count adjacent symbol pair frequencies across the vocabulary."""
    pairs: Dict[Tuple[str, str], int] = defaultdict(int)
    for word, freq in vocab.items():
        symbols = word.split()
        for i in range(len(symbols) - 1):
            pairs[(symbols[i], symbols[i + 1])] += freq
    return dict(pairs)


def merge_vocab(pair: Tuple[str, str], vocab: Dict[str, int]) -> Dict[str, int]:
    """Merge all occurrences of  in the vocabulary."""
    import re
    bigram = re.escape(" ".join(pair))
    pattern = re.compile(r"(?<!\S)" + bigram + r"(?!\S)")
    new_vocab: Dict[str, int] = {}
    for word, freq in vocab.items():
        new_word = pattern.sub("".join(pair), word)
        new_vocab[new_word] = freq
    return new_vocab


def train_bpe(corpus: str, num_merges: int) -> List[Tuple[str, str]]:
    """Train BPE and return the ordered list of merge rules."""
    vocab = get_vocab(corpus)
    merges: List[Tuple[str, str]] = []
    for i in range(num_merges):
        pairs = get_stats(vocab)
        if not pairs:
            break
        best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)
        vocab = merge_vocab(best, vocab)
        merges.append(best)
        print(f"Merge {i+1}: {best[0]!r} + {best[1]!r} -> {''.join(best)!r}")
    return merges


# Demo
corpus = "low low low lower lower lowest newest new new new wider wide"
merges = train_bpe(corpus, num_merges=10)
print("
Learned merges:", merges)
library provides fast Rust-backed BPE tokenization for GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 models. It is the tokenizer used when calling the OpenAI API and is useful for accurate token counting before sending prompts:

import tiktoken

# Load the encoding used by GPT-4
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 / GPT-3.5-turbo

text = "unhappiness is a compound word"
tokens = enc.encode(text)
print("Token IDs:", tokens)
print("Token count:", len(tokens))

# Decode individual tokens for inspection
for tok_id in tokens:
    print(repr(enc.decode([tok_id])), "→", tok_id)

# Count tokens for a chat message (accounting for overhead)
def count_tokens_for_chat(messages, model="gpt-4") -> int:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    tokens_per_message = 3   # every message has <|im_start|>, role, 

    tokens_per_name = 1
    total = 0
    for msg in messages:
        total += tokens_per_message
        for key, value in msg.items():
            total += len(enc.encode(value))
            if key == "name":
                total += tokens_per_name
    total += 3               # reply prime
    return total

msgs = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user",   "content": "Explain subword tokenization."},
]
print("Chat token count:", count_tokens_for_chat(msgs))

Meta's LLaMA 3 (2024) uses a SentencePiece tokenizer trained with the Byte Fallback BPE algorithm on approximately 15 trillion tokens of multilingual text. The vocabulary size was increased from 32 000 (LLaMA 1/2) to 128 000 tokens specifically to improve representation of non-Latin scripts and reduce fragmentation for Korean, Arabic, and Indic languages. At 128k tokens, LLaMA 3's tokenizer encodes English text at roughly 3.7 characters per token, while LLaMA 2's 32k vocabulary achieved only ~3.3 — a direct improvement in context-window efficiency.

encoding — a byte-level BPE tokenizer with 100 277 tokens. The vocabulary includes dedicated tokens for common Unicode emoji and code constructs, which keeps Python, JavaScript, and SQL code tokenized at near-word-level granularity and improves code-completion accuracy compared to the older

Both examples share the same deployment architecture: the tokenizer is a standalone artifact (a JSON or protobuf file) distributed separately from the model weights. At inference, a Rust or C++ tokenizer library decodes at 1–5 MB/s of text per CPU core, so tokenization latency is negligible compared to the model's forward pass. The tokenizer artifact is version-locked to the model: switching model versions requires switching tokenizer versions in lockstep, which is a common source of silent bugs in production systems.
token IDs with a model expecting

IDs, or any other tokenizer mismatch, silently produces wrong outputs because the model interprets each integer ID against its own embedding table. Always load the tokenizer using the model name, not by hardcoding an encoding string:

# Wrong: hardcoding an encoding that might not match the model
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("p50k_base")  # dangerous if model is GPT-4

# Correct: derive the encoding from the model name
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")  # resolves to cl100k_base
Ignoring the continuation prefix in WordPiece
BERT's WordPiece tokenizer marks continuation tokens with a
). When reconstructing text from token strings, stripping
method introduces spurious spaces. Always use the official decode method.

A common pattern is to truncate text to N characters and assume the result fits in M tokens. This is unreliable: short characters can be many tokens if they belong to a script with high fragmentation. Count tokens
GPT-2 and GPT-4 tokenizers absorb leading whitespace into the following token (
Pin the tokenizer version alongside the model version in your dependency manifest. Treat them as a coupled artifact — a tokenizer upgrade is a model upgrade.
Always tokenize to measure before submitting to an API. Off-by-one token errors near the context limit silently truncate content or trigger unexpected charges.
For multilingual applications, choose a vocabulary trained on balanced multilingual data (or explicitly on your target languages) rather than re-using an English-dominant vocabulary.
by default when calling Transformers tokenizers; many models require specific BOS/EOS tokens and misbehave without them.
to at least 2 to avoid overfitting merge rules to corpus artefacts.
If authoring a new tokenizer, prefer byte-level BPE over character-level BPE because byte-level guarantees lossless encoding of any UTF-8 string without a special UNK fallback.
When training for code generation, include a representative code corpus during tokenizer training — otherwise common identifiers and keywords fragment unnecessarily.
is the target vocabulary size and

is the number of distinct word types in the corpus. For a typical LLM corpus of hundreds of billions of tokens, training the tokenizer on a representative sample of a few gigabytes takes minutes on a single CPU using the Hugging Face
Encoding throughput at inference time is the more operationally critical metric. The

Rust implementation encodes at approximately 5–15 MB/s per core on typical text. At a median of ~4 bytes per token, that corresponds to 1.25–3.75 million tokens per second per core — fast enough to never be the bottleneck in an LLM serving stack where the model forward pass processes only thousands of tokens per second.
Vocabulary size has a direct impact on model memory and training speed: the embedding matrix has shape
and the output projection (lm_head) has shape
. For a 128k vocabulary and a 4096-dimensional model, these two layers together require
in bfloat16 — a non-trivial fraction of total model parameters. Larger vocabularies also add to the softmax cost in cross-entropy loss during training.

Subword tokenization is embarrassingly parallelisable at inference because each sequence can be tokenized independently. Batching strategies that bucket sequences by length before tokenization reduce padding overhead and improve GPU utilisation downstream.
(Rust + Python): the canonical library for training and using BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram tokenizers. Supports byte-level BPE, normalisation pipelines, and pre-tokenisation hooks.

SentencePiece (Google): C++ library with Python bindings for Unigram and BPE models. Used by T5, ALBERT, LLaMA 1/2, Mistral, and Gemma. Handles whitespace differently (no pre-tokenisation by whitespace, uses
tiktoken (OpenAI): fast Rust/Python BPE library used for GPT-2 through GPT-4. Pre-built vocabularies:
: a unified API that wraps any of the above libraries and loads the correct tokenizer for a given model checkpoint from the Hub.
NLTK / spaCy: older word-level and rule-based tokenizers still used for classical NLP tasks and pre-LLM pipelines, not for LLM subword tokenization.

# Load any model's tokenizer with a single line
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B")

text = "Subword tokenization handles neologisms gracefully."
ids = tokenizer.encode(text)
print(ids)
print([tokenizer.decode([i]) for i in ids])

# SentencePiece directly
import sentencepiece as spm

sp = spm.SentencePieceProcessor()
sp.Load("llama.model")   # path to the .model file
pieces = sp.EncodeAsPieces("unhappiness")
print(pieces)           # ['▁un', 'happi', 'ness']
ids = sp.EncodeAsIds("unhappiness")
print(ids)
The table below compares the three mainstream subword algorithms and the two classical approaches across the dimensions most relevant for LLM development.
Tokenization is an unexpected attack surface in LLM deployments. Several practical risks deserve attention:

Because the tokenizer operates independently of any content-filtering layer, an attacker can craft strings that, at the character level, appear to contain blocked keywords but tokenize into a completely different set of token IDs. Conversely, certain Unicode homoglyphs or zero-width characters can cause a string to fragment in ways that defeat keyword-based safety checks applied before tokenization. Safety filters must operate on token IDs (post-tokenization) or use a normalised string representation, not on raw bytes.

Inputs constructed from rare Unicode code points — for instance, long sequences of CJK characters or mixed-script strings — can generate far more tokens than their byte length suggests when run through a primarily English-trained tokenizer. A 1 000-byte input might produce 4 000 tokens, exhausting the context window and inflating API costs. Validate token count after encoding, not after character-count limiting.

A tokenizer change between model versions is a silent breaking change. Production systems should record the tokenizer version (or its SHA-256 hash) alongside every stored embedding or cached tokenization result. Re-tokenizing stored text after a tokenizer upgrade can change token IDs even for identical strings if merge rules changed.

Think of the BPE vocabulary as a compression codebook built from the inside out. Start with a grammar that only knows individual letters. Every time you find a pair of adjacent symbols that frequently appear together in the training text, you invent a new symbol to represent them as a unit — just like abbreviating "United States" to "US" once you notice you need to write it thousands of times. After enough iterations, common words and morphemes have their own symbols, rare words are spelled out from smaller pieces, and every possible string is encodable because individual characters are always available as fallback symbols. The model then learns meaning in terms of these abbreviation units, which is why two words that share a morpheme (

The Unigram variant adds a probabilistic twist: instead of a fixed abbreviation codebook, it maintains a probability distribution over all possible segmentations and can sample alternative tokenizations — a form of data augmentation that regularises the model by preventing it from committing too hard to any single decomposition.
Subword tokenization splits text into variable-length units between characters and words, achieving open vocabulary, manageable sequence lengths, and morphological sharing in a single mechanism.
BPE trains by iteratively merging the most frequent adjacent symbol pair; WordPiece normalises by marginal frequency; Unigram LM uses Viterbi-optimal segmentation with probabilistic sampling.
Byte-level BPE guarantees zero OOV rate for any UTF-8 input by treating raw bytes as the base vocabulary.
The tokenizer is version-locked to its model — treating them as separate, independently upgradable components is a common source of silent production bugs.
Vocabulary size is a design trade-off: larger vocabularies improve encoding efficiency and multilingual coverage but increase embedding-layer memory and softmax cost.
Always measure token count after encoding, not by estimating from character count — high-fragmentation inputs can produce far more tokens than their byte length implies.
Safety and content-filtering logic must operate post-tokenization or on normalised strings to avoid bypasses via Unicode homoglyphs or fragmentation artefacts.
library, SentencePiece, and tiktoken are the three primary production-grade implementations, each suited to different model families.
